People often rely on underlying theories or frameworks to interpret evidence and make sense of the world around them. These theories can serve as cognitive shortcuts, helping individuals process information efficiently and make decisions. When presented with evidence that contradicts their existing beliefs or theories, individuals may be resistant to change because it disrupts their understanding of the world. In such situations, having a robust underlying theory that accommodates the new evidence can make it easier for people to accept and integrate new information into their worldview.

I learned that even if you have a lot of solid, strong evidences for the topic of discussion, people will not really trust you unless you bring out a well established theory that supports your opinion.

Back in the 19th century there was a large gap (3 to 1) in deaths at childbirth between two hospitals. A doctor then performed tests and found that the problem was that the doctors didn't wash their hands before the procedure. When he told them his findings, nobody heard him, but only decades later, when tests regarding bacteria were published, they changed their operating process. It proves that the scientific community was very strict back then, regarding their published studies, vs. the studies of a single person, even though he had performed all the necessary tests.
